summary: Italian Renaissance/Mannerist painter from Parma. Known for illusionistic ceiling frescoes using di sotto in su technique. Most commonly identified by Assumption of the Virgin (Parma Cathedral), the Loves of Jupiter series (Jupiter and Io, Danaë, Abduction of Ganymede, Leda and the Swan), and Camera di San Paolo frescoes. Teacher of Parmigianino.

works.name: Assumption of the Virgin (Parma Cathedral)
works.description: Illusionistic ceiling fresco using di sotto in su technique. Spiraling layers of clouds disguise octagonal architecture. Figures appear to jump toward the viewer. Wikipedia

works.name: Loves of Jupiter series
works.description: Four-painting series commissioned by Federico II Gonzaga. Includes Jupiter and Io (cloud with humanoid face embraces woman), Danaë (shower of gold), Abduction of Ganymede (eagle carries youth), Leda and the Swan.

recursive_suggestions: Di sotto in su technique — cross-topic with Mantegna
comprehensive_summary:
Correggio (Antonio Allegri da Correggio) was an Italian Renaissance/Mannerist painter from Parma, best known for illusionistic ceiling frescoes using the di sotto in su technique. His Assumption of the Virgin in Parma Cathedral features spiraling layers of clouds that disguise the octagonal architecture, with figures appearing to jump toward the viewer.

His Loves of Jupiter series, commissioned by Federico II Gonzaga, includes four paintings: Jupiter and Io (a stormy cloud with a humanoid face embraces a woman from behind), Danaë (an angel looks toward a shower of gold), Abduction of Ganymede (an eagle carries the youth), and Leda and the Swan. He painted Diana over a fireplace in the Camera di San Paolo (abbess's bedroom). His Vision of St. John on Patmos pairs gospel writers with Doctors of the Church. He was the teacher of Parmigianino and helped complete Mantegna's family chapel in Mantua.
